as Kent Beck.definition: Adopt Kent Beck's pragmatic, test-first, iterative stance: the response favors simplicity, working code, and small steps over elaboration.

distinctions.note: as Kent Beck = specifically XP/TDD simplicity philosophy; as programmer = general engineering voice without that specific philosophy

as PM.definition: Adopt a product manager's stance focused on outcomes and scope: the response foregrounds user value, trade-offs, and stakeholder alignment.

distinctions.note: voice:as PM = output speaks FROM a PM stance; audience:to product manager = output is addressed TO a PM reader
as PM = outcomes, scope, stakeholder value; as designer = UX, interaction, visual clarity

as designer.definition: Adopt a designer's stance focused on usability and interaction: the response involves UX decisions, flows, or visual clarity.

distinctions.note: voice:as designer = output speaks FROM a designer stance; audience:to designer = output is addressed TO a designer reader
as designer = UX, interaction, visual clarity; as PM = outcomes, scope, stakeholder alignment

as facilitator.definition: Adopt a facilitator's stance that guides process: the response balances voices, structures participation, and maintains momentum.

distinctions.note: as facilitator = guides process and participation; as teacher = transmits content and builds understanding

as junior engineer.definition: Adopt a junior engineer's curious, candid stance: the response surfaces questions, acknowledges uncertainty, and shows its work.
heuristics: think aloud as a junior dev

distinctions.note: as junior engineer = curiosity, candor, surfacing uncertainty; as programmer = confident technical precision
as junior engineer = candid, learning-oriented; as principal engineer = broad systems thinking, trade-off authority

as principal engineer.definition: Adopt a principal engineer's systems-thinking stance: the response brings architectural breadth, trade-off reasoning, and pragmatic guidance.

distinctions.note: as principal engineer = systems thinking, architectural breadth, trade-offs; as programmer = implementation-focused engineering precision
as principal engineer = authoritative breadth; as junior engineer = candid, questioning, learning-oriented

as programmer.definition: Adopt a programmer's technical stance: the response reasons and explains like an engineer — precise, implementation-minded, direct.

distinctions.note: voice:as programmer = output speaks FROM a programmer stance; audience:to programmer = output is addressed TO a programmer reader
as programmer = general engineering voice; as principal engineer = systems-thinking, trade-off, architectural breadth

as prompt engineer.definition: Adopt a prompt-engineering stance: the response involves designing, critiquing, or refining prompts explicitly.

distinctions.note: as prompt engineer = stance is about prompt design and LLM interaction; as programmer = stance is about code and implementation

as scientist.definition: Adopt a scientific, evidence-first stance: the response foregrounds hypotheses, evidence, and rigor.

distinctions.note: as scientist = evidence-first, hypothesis-driven rigor; as programmer = implementation-minded, engineering precision

as teacher.definition: Adopt a teacher's stance that scaffolds understanding: the response breaks concepts down gradually for a learner.

distinctions.note: as teacher = builds understanding through instruction; as facilitator = guides process and participation without teaching
as teacher = pedagogy and scaffolding; as writer = prose craft and narrative flow

as writer.definition: Adopt a writer's stance focused on narrative clarity: the response involves prose craftsmanship, storytelling, and communication flow.

distinctions.note: as writer = craft of prose and narrative; as teacher = scaffolded explanation and pedagogy

to CEO.definition: Address a CEO with business impact and crisp asks: the audience is a CEO or C-suite executive who needs crisp business framing.

distinctions.note: to CEO = C-suite, business impact framing; to managers = operational managers, risk and results
to CEO = single executive, decisive framing; to stakeholders = mixed group, broad clarity on what matters

to Kent Beck.definition: Address Kent Beck's values — concrete, test-minded, iterative: the audience values small batches, working code, and simplicity.

distinctions.note: to Kent Beck = specifically Kent Beck's philosophy; to XP enthusiast = general XP practitioner audience

to LLM.definition: Address another language model: output will be consumed by another LLM — make it explicit, unambiguous, and free of prose fluff.

distinctions.note: to LLM = output consumed by a language model, precision and no prose fluff; to programmer = output consumed by a human developer

to XP enthusiast.definition: Address an XP enthusiast who values social programming and production validation: the audience practices small batches, pair/mob, and continuous delivery.

distinctions.note: to XP enthusiast = general XP community; to Kent Beck = specifically Kent Beck's philosophy and style

to analyst.definition: Address an analyst with structured, data-framed output: the audience needs evidence, metrics, and structured results for further analysis.

distinctions.note: to analyst = data, structure, metrics; to programmer = implementation code, technical precision

to designer.definition: Address a designer with UX and visual clarity framing: the audience cares about user flows, interaction patterns, and design rationale.

distinctions.note: audience:to designer = output addressed TO a designer reader; voice:as designer = output speaks FROM a designer perspective

to junior engineer.definition: Address a junior engineer with clear, encouraging guidance: the audience needs thorough explanation and supportive tone.
heuristics: explain to someone new to this

distinctions.note: to junior engineer = thorough, scaffolded, encouraging; to programmer = concise, implementation-ready, assumes competence
to junior engineer = explain, scaffold, encourage; to principal engineer = concise, architectural, minimal hand-holding

to managers.definition: Address managers focused on outcomes and risk: the audience cares about staffing, risk, and results rather than technical implementation.

distinctions.note: to managers = specific management layer, operational ownership; to stakeholders = mixed roles, cross-functional impact clarity
to managers = operational managers; to CEO = C-suite, business impact, crisp asks

to platform team.definition: Address a platform team focused on reliability and paved paths: the audience cares about leverage, reliability contracts, and making the right thing easy.

distinctions.note: to platform team = reliability contracts, paved paths, leverage; to stream aligned team = flow, local ownership, delivery speed

to principal engineer.definition: Address a principal engineer with concise, architectural framing: the audience is a senior technical leader who wants trade-offs and minimal hand-holding.

distinctions.note: to principal engineer = architectural, trade-offs, systems thinking; to programmer = implementation-focused
to principal engineer = concise, assume expertise; to junior engineer = thorough, scaffold, encourage

to product manager.definition: Address a product manager with scope and user value framing: the audience is a PM connecting user needs to scope decisions.

distinctions.note: to product manager = scope, user value, product decisions; to managers = operational outcomes, risk, staffing

to programmer.definition: Address a programmer with technical, implementation-ready output: the audience is a developer who expects precision and wants to act directly.

distinctions.note: audience:to programmer = output addressed TO a programmer reader; voice:as programmer = output speaks FROM an engineering perspective
to programmer = concise, assumes competence; to junior engineer = scaffolded, encouraging

to stakeholders.definition: Address a broad stakeholder group focused on impact and decisions: the audience includes mixed roles needing clarity on what matters and why.

distinctions.note: to stakeholders = mixed roles, cross-functional clarity; to managers = specific management layer
to stakeholders = external or cross-org clarity; to team = immediate team, actionable and collaborative

to stream aligned team.definition: Address a stream-aligned team focused on flow and local ownership: the audience cares about delivery speed, reducing dependencies, and owning their domain.

distinctions.note: to stream aligned team = flow, delivery, local ownership; to platform team = reliability, paved paths, leverage

to team.definition: Address your own team with actionable, collaborative framing: direct, implementation-ready communication.

distinctions.note: to team = immediate team, collaborative and direct; to stakeholders = mixed external roles, impact-focused

casually.definition: Casual, conversational register: use when formality would feel stiff or the subject benefits from a relaxed tone.

distinctions.note: casually = relaxed, conversational; formally = professional, structured, appropriate for leadership or compliance
casually = informal register; directly = no-hedging register (can be casual or formal)

directly.definition: Direct, no-hedging register: the user wants a straight answer without softening or qualifications.

distinctions.note: directly = no hedging or softening (orthogonal to formality); casually = informal register
directly = no softening; gently = supportive, sensitive framing for difficult subjects

formally.definition: Formal, professional register: use when the output will be shared with leadership, external parties, or compliance contexts.

distinctions.note: formally = structured, professional; casually = relaxed, conversational
formally = register (level of formality); directly = hedging style (can be formal or informal)

gently.definition: Gentle, supportive register: use when the subject involves sensitive feedback, interpersonal difficulty, or someone who needs encouragement.

distinctions.note: gently = sensitive, supportive for difficult or emotional subjects; kindly = warm alongside substance for coaching or junior audiences
gently = softens delivery; directly = no softening

kindly.definition: Kind, warm register: the response conveys warmth alongside substance — for coaching, junior audiences, or emotionally charged contexts.

distinctions.note: kindly = warmth and positive regard alongside substance; gently = protective softening for difficult or sensitive subjects

announce.definition: Communicate news or a change: the goal is to share a decision, launch, or update with an audience.

distinctions.note: announce = news or change event, often one-time; inform = general knowledge transfer or status update

appreciate.definition: Communicate gratitude or recognition: the goal is to acknowledge contribution, celebrate work, or express thanks.

distinctions.note: appreciate = pure recognition and gratitude; coach = developmental, growth-oriented

coach.definition: Communicate to develop the audience: the goal is growth, capability building, or guiding someone through a challenge.

distinctions.note: coach = growth, capability building, challenge navigation; teach = build conceptual understanding
coach = develop capability; persuade = move toward a position

inform.definition: Communicate to transfer knowledge or update understanding: the goal is to give the audience the information they need.

distinctions.note: inform = transfer facts or status; teach = build internalized understanding of concepts
inform = neutral knowledge transfer; persuade = move the audience toward a view or decision

persuade.definition: Communicate to influence belief or action: the goal is to move the audience toward a view or decision.

distinctions.note: persuade = move toward a view or decision; inform = neutral knowledge transfer
persuade = advocate for a position; coach = develop capability and guide through challenges

teach.definition: Communicate to build understanding: the goal is learning and the audience needs to internalize concepts, not just receive information.

distinctions.note: teach = internalization of concepts; inform = transfer facts or status
teach = build conceptual understanding; coach = develop capability and guide through challenges

designer_to_pm.definition: Design decisions communicated to a product manager: explains trade-offs and UX rationale to a PM audience.
heuristics: UX decision for a product manager

distinctions.note: designer_to_pm = design explaining to PM; product_manager_to_team = PM explaining to engineering/design team

executive_brief.definition: Concise high-stakes summary for a CEO or executive: surfaces business impact, risk, and crisp asks in direct language.

distinctions.note: executive_brief = single C-suite recipient, crisp and decisive; stakeholder_facilitator = mixed group, facilitating alignment

fun_mode.definition: Casual, playful tone across the board: the subject calls for levity and an entertaining register.

distinctions.note: fun_mode = levity and playfulness as the primary register; peer_engineer_explanation = technical substance with peer framing

peer_engineer_explanation.definition: Technical explanation to a fellow engineer: engineer-to-engineer framing for a programmer or peer engineer.

distinctions.note: peer_engineer_explanation = peer-level, assumes competence; teach_junior_dev = scaffolded, encouraging for a learner

product_manager_to_team.definition: Product direction communicated to the team: a PM frames product goals or retrospective insights for the engineering or design team.

distinctions.note: product_manager_to_team = PM explaining to team; designer_to_pm = designer explaining to PM

scientist_to_analyst.definition: Evidence-based analysis presented formally to an analyst: rigorous structure, data framing, and formal tone.

distinctions.note: scientist_to_analyst = evidence-based, data-structured, formal; peer_engineer_explanation = implementation-oriented, engineering voice

stakeholder_facilitator.definition: Driving alignment with mixed stakeholders: helps a facilitator guide a group toward a decision.

distinctions.note: stakeholder_facilitator = mixed group, process-oriented alignment; executive_brief = single C-suite recipient, crisp business asks

teach_junior_dev.definition: Mentoring or onboarding a junior developer: patient, scaffolded explanation with encouragement.
heuristics: kind clear explanation for someone new

distinctions.note: teach_junior_dev = scaffolded, encouraging, patient; peer_engineer_explanation = peer-level, assumes competence

patterns.title: Decision-Making
patterns.command: bar build diff thing full branch variants --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build diff thing full branch variants --subject "Choose between Redis and Postgres for caching"
patterns.desc: Use when choosing between options or evaluating alternatives

patterns.title: Architecture Documentation
patterns.command: bar build make struct full explore case --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build make struct full explore case --subject "Document the microservices architecture"
patterns.desc: Use for creating ADRs or documenting architectural decisions

patterns.title: Explanation/Understanding (Process)
patterns.command: bar build show time full flow walkthrough --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build show time full flow walkthrough --subject "Explain the OAuth authentication flow"
patterns.desc: Use when explaining how something works over time or in sequence

patterns.title: Explanation/Understanding (Concepts)
patterns.command: bar build show mean full scaffold --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build show mean full scaffold --subject "What is eventual consistency?"
patterns.desc: Use when explaining what something means or building conceptual understanding

patterns.title: Structural Analysis
patterns.command: bar build probe struct full mapping --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe struct full mapping --subject "Analyze the database schema relationships"
patterns.desc: Use for understanding relationships, boundaries, and structure

patterns.title: Problem Diagnosis
patterns.command: bar build probe fail full diagnose checklist --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe fail full diagnose checklist --subject "Debug production memory leak"
patterns.desc: Use for troubleshooting and root cause analysis

patterns.title: Task Planning
patterns.command: bar build plan act full converge actions --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build plan act full converge actions --subject "Plan the database migration steps"
patterns.desc: Use when breaking down work into actionable steps

patterns.title: Exploratory Analysis
patterns.command: bar build probe thing full explore variants --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe thing full explore variants --subject "What are different approaches to state management?"
patterns.desc: Use when surveying possibilities or generating alternatives

patterns.title: Comparison/Tradeoff Analysis
patterns.command: bar build diff thing full table --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build diff thing full table --subject "Compare REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC for our API"
patterns.desc: Use for side-by-side comparison of alternatives with tradeoffs

patterns.title: Risk Analysis
patterns.command: bar build probe fail full adversarial checklist --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe fail full adversarial checklist --subject "Assess the risk posture of migrating to Kubernetes"
patterns.desc: Use for open-ended risk analysis: 'how risky is this?' or 'assess failure posture'

patterns.title: Risk Extraction
patterns.command: bar build pull fail full risks checklist --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build pull fail full risks checklist --subject "Deploy payment service on Friday"
patterns.desc: Use when extracting a bounded risk list or summary: 'what are the risks?'. Prefer pull over probe when a risk register or checklist is the deliverable, not an open-ended analysis.

patterns.title: Quality Evaluation
patterns.command: bar build check good full analysis checklist --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build check good full analysis checklist --subject "Evaluate code review quality standards"
patterns.desc: Use when assessing quality, standards, or success criteria

patterns.title: Progressive Refinement Workflow
patterns.command: bar build probe thing gist explore variants --subject "..." && bar build probe struct full mapping table --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe thing gist explore variants --subject "API design approaches" && bar build probe struct full mapping table --subject "Selected REST API structure"
patterns.desc: Use for multi-step workflows: explore broadly, then analyze deeply

patterns.title: Conceptual Scaffolding
patterns.command: bar build show mean full scaffold --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build show mean full scaffold --subject "Explain CQRS pattern for beginners"
patterns.desc: Use for building understanding from fundamentals to complex concepts

patterns.title: Failure Mode Analysis
patterns.command: bar build probe fail full adversarial variants --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe fail full adversarial variants --subject "How could the payment system fail under load?"
patterns.desc: Use for systematic analysis of how systems can break

patterns.title: Success Criteria Definition
patterns.command: bar build make good full analysis checklist --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build make good full analysis checklist --subject "Define success criteria for the dashboard redesign"
patterns.desc: Use when establishing measurable quality or success standards

patterns.title: Perspective Analysis
patterns.command: bar build probe view full explore variants --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe view full explore variants --subject "How do different stakeholders view the monolith migration?"
patterns.desc: Use for understanding multiple viewpoints or stakeholder perspectives

patterns.title: Impact Assessment
patterns.command: bar build probe struct full effects table --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe struct full effects table --subject "Assess downstream impacts of changing the auth service"
patterns.desc: Use for analyzing ripple effects and dependencies

patterns.title: Constraint Mapping
patterns.command: bar build probe thing full dimension table --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe thing full dimension table --subject "Map technical and business constraints for the mobile app"
patterns.desc: Use for identifying and documenting limitations and requirements

patterns.title: Evidence Building
patterns.command: bar build make thing full cite case --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build make thing full cite case --subject "Build the case for adopting TypeScript"
patterns.desc: Use when making a persuasive argument with supporting evidence

patterns.title: Option Generation with Reasoning
patterns.command: bar build probe thing full branch variants --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe thing full branch variants --subject "Generate database sharding approaches with pros/cons"
patterns.desc: Use for generating alternatives with detailed reasoning for each

patterns.title: Sequential Process Documentation
patterns.command: bar build make time full flow recipe --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build make time full flow recipe --subject "Document the CI/CD pipeline stages"
patterns.desc: Use for documenting step-by-step processes or workflows

patterns.title: Scenario Simulation
patterns.command: bar build sim time full walkthrough --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build sim time full walkthrough --subject "Simulate what happens during a database failover"
patterns.desc: Use for playing out hypothetical or contingency scenarios

patterns.title: Dependency Analysis
patterns.command: bar build probe struct full depends mapping --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe struct full depends mapping --subject "Map service dependencies in the microservices architecture"
patterns.desc: Use for understanding and visualizing dependencies and relationships

patterns.title: Summarisation / Extraction
patterns.command: bar build pull gist mean --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build pull gist mean --subject "[long RFC or design document]"
patterns.desc: Use when compressing a long source document into a shorter summary. Prefer pull over show when a SUBJECT document is being compressed: pull extracts a subset, show explains a concept. Heuristic: long SUBJECT to compress → pull; concept to explain without a source → show.

patterns.title: Test Coverage Gap Analysis
patterns.command: bar build check fail full checklist --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build check fail full checklist --subject "Feature: user registration flow"
patterns.desc: Use when identifying missing tests or coverage gaps in existing code. Heuristic: 'what tests are missing?' → check; 'write a test plan' → make.

patterns.title: Test Plan Creation
patterns.command: bar build make act fail full checklist --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build make act fail full checklist --subject "Payment integration feature"
patterns.desc: Use when creating a new test plan or test cases from scratch. Produces a test plan artifact rather than evaluating existing coverage.

patterns.title: Pre-mortem / Inversion Exercise
patterns.command: bar build probe fail full inversion variants --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build probe fail full inversion variants --subject "Our Q4 launch plan"
patterns.desc: Use when assuming failure and working backward to identify causes. Frames the exercise as: 'assume this has failed — what went wrong?' Pairs naturally with planning and architecture review tasks.

patterns.title: Comprehensive Assessment (Multi-Scope)
patterns.command: bar build check <scope> full <method> --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build check good full analysis --subject "Assess codebase quality"
patterns.desc: Use for multi-faceted assessments that span quality (good), fragility (fail), and structure (struct). When the task requires multiple analytical lenses, prioritize by primary concern or analyze sequentially: quality-first (good), risk-first (fail), or architecture-first (struct).

patterns.title: Evaluation with Falsification
patterns.command: bar build check <scope> full verify risks --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build check thing full verify risks --subject "Evaluate the proposed caching strategy"
patterns.desc: Use when evaluating claims by actively searching for ways they could be wrong. Combines verify (falsification pressure) with risks (systematic problem identification). Best for: reviewing designs, validating assumptions, stress-testing proposals.

patterns.title: Plain Prose Output
patterns.command: bar build show <scope> full plain --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build show mean full plain --subject "Explain the authorization model"
patterns.desc: Use when the response must be plain prose — no lists, bullets, or tables. The plain channel explicitly suppresses structural decoration. Heuristic: 'no bullets', 'no formatting', 'plain prose', 'flowing paragraphs' → add plain channel to any task.

patterns.title: Synchronous Session Plan
patterns.command: bar build plan act full sync --subject "..."
patterns.example: bar build plan act full sync --subject "Design sprint kickoff — 3h with context, problem framing, and ideation"
patterns.desc: Use when the output should be a synchronous session plan with agenda, timing slots, and facilitation cues for real-time use. Heuristic: 'session plan', 'live workshop', 'meeting agenda with timing', 'facilitation script for live session' → sync channel. Combine with facilitate form when facilitator role is explicit.
